title: Holoprosencephaly -- Deterrence and Patient Education
content: Genetic testing is recommended for parents with children showing apparent symptoms or for those showing mild variants. [12] However, the indication for testing depends on the disease's clinical suspicion, which is very difficult given the numerous phenotypic variations. The number of genes involved and a multi-hit process compound the challenging diagnosis further. The de-novo occurrence of some mutations that show a specific defect in the parents has also been observed. In these cases, the chances of HPE in a subsequent pregnancy are significantly reduced. [24]

content: Huntington disease, neuroacanthocytosis, and Wilson disease are genetic disorders. Hence, counseling should be provided to any couple who plans to conceive, especially if one partner has the disorder. Genetic testing should be offered to any family member if they want to know the potential risk of inheriting the disease. All patients must be educated regarding the clinical features of the disease, as well as the treatment options and prognosis.

title: Biochemistry, Protein Targeting and I Cell Diseases -- Testing -- Prenatal Diagnosis
content: The autosomal recessive nature of ICD means a higher risk of inheritance among consanguineous families. In a case series performed by Okada et al, out of the 18 families had consanguinity. Five families were first cousins, 2 were second cousins, and 1 family had grandmothers who were both first cousins. [55] [56] Manifestations of ICD are recognized at birth, with death occurring within the first decade of life at an average of 5 years. [57] [58] The short lifespan warrants prenatal diagnosis. The decision to undergo the procedures accompanying prenatal diagnosis is associated with their complications. Hence, they are usually performed on a family with a previous history of having babies with ICD. [58]

title: Hydranencephaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
content: It is extremely important to differentiate between hydranencephaly and severe-extreme hydrocephalus. Both disorders can present similarly, but prognosis and management are very different. A patient with severe-extreme hydrocephalus improves significantly clinically and radiographically after treatment, while those with hydranencephaly do not. If treatment is delayed, it can lead to catastrophic outcomes.

title: Ciliary Dysfunction -- Evaluation
content: Additionally, false positives can occur in all the above testing methods due to secondary loss of cilia function. This can occur, for example, if the sample was taken during an acute viral illness that caused transient loss of function. Cell culture can be used to minimize this impact. [25] Genetic testing should also be considered, as 33 genes have been associated with primary ciliary dyskinesia. However, not all of them are included in the commercially available primary ciliary dyskinesia genetic testing panel. As genetic panels continue to develop and advance, genetic testing continues to be a valued test for diagnosis and research. Currently, however, there remain limitations as known PCD patients can have normal genetic testing. [16] In the United States, a combination of ciliary biopsy to demonstrate ciliary ultrastructural abnormalities and genetic testing is used for the diagnosis. In contrast, a combination of genetic testing and high-speed video microscopy is used in Europe.

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is recognized as a neurological disorder that involves the brain and nerves, and it is specifically identified within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway where excess cerebrospinal fluid accumulates. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with the development of dementia (خرف). In the management of hydrocephalus, shunting procedures are commonly employed; the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) frequently co‑occurs with the condition and represents a specific type of shunt that diverts fluid from a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site. This shunt is a child concept of the broader category “shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is nested under the procedural domain of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. Another variant, the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, is performed directly on the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway and is considered closely related, and possibly synonymous, with the general insertion/replacement/removal procedure for extracranial ventricular shunts. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose hydrocephalus, anticipate its cognitive complications, and select appropriate shunting interventions for optimal patient care.

content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is classified as a member of the Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term) and falls within the broader domain of Brain and Nerves, reflecting its impact on cranial structures such as hlava (head). Clinical assessment of hydrocephalus commonly involves the Brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway, which serves both as the finding site for the condition and as the direct procedural site for interventions like a ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx. Therapeutic management frequently includes a Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site; the specific procedure known as التحويلة البطينية البريتونية (ventriculoperitoneal shunt) is a child of this category. Both the Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site and the Ventricular shunt to nasopharynx are encompassed by the procedural umbrella of insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt and are considered related or possibly synonymous. Hydrocephalus therefore co‑occurs with التحويلة البطينية البريتونية, highlighting the common use of ventriculoperitoneal shunting in its treatment.
